h1Title: Late Lesions of Pinta: Causes, Skin Manifestations & Management
heroOverview: Pinta (also known as carate, mal del pinto, or azul) is a chronic non-venereal treponemal infection caused by Treponema carateum, a spirochaete indistinguishable clinically and serologically from the causative agents of yaws (T. pallidum subspecies pertenue) and syphilis (T. pallidum subspecies pallidum). The disease is limited to remote rural communities in Central and South America, transmitted by direct skin-to-skin contact in non-sexual settings. Pinta progresses through three stages: primary (pintides, a slowly healing skin ulcer), secondary (depigmented skin patches), and late/chronic (dyschromic changes with pigmentary alteration and atrophic skin changes). Late lesions of pinta occur years to decades after initial infection and are characterised by achromia (loss of pigment), hyperchromia (excess pigment), and hyperkeratotic plaques that may be disfiguring. Unlike syphilis, pinta does not involve the cardiovascular or nervous systems. Benzathine penicillin G remains the treatment of choice, with excellent cure rates even in late-stage disease.

keyStats.prevalence: Endemic only in remote rural communities of Central and South America; estimated prevalence in endemic areas 1-5% in active foci; most cases reported from Mexico, Colombia, Venezuela, Ecuador, Peru, and Brazil
keyStats.demographics: Affects children and young adults living in remote, underserved communities with limited healthcare access; equal sex distribution; no sexual transmission
keyStats.avgAge: Primary lesions typically appear in childhood (ages 5-15); late lesions manifest in adolescence or adulthood (15-40 years) years after initial infection
keyStats.globalCases: Few thousand cases reported annually; likely significantly under-reported due to geographic isolation and limited surveillance in remote communities

definition: Late lesions of pinta (ICD-10: A67.2, late stage of pinta / dyschromic skin lesions) represent the chronic phase of pinta infection occurring years to decades after primary infection. The causative organism, Treponema carateum, is transmitted by direct person-to-person contact (non-venereal) in conditions of poverty, poor hygiene, and overcrowding. After the primary ulcer (pintide) heals and secondary depigmented lesions fade, late-stage manifestations develop in approximately 50% of untreated patients, characterised by pigmentary disturbance (achromic and hyperchromic patches), hyperkeratotic and atrophic skin changes, and lymphocytic infiltration of the dermis. The disease is non-venereal, does not involve the cardiovascular or nervous systems, and does not cause congenital infection. Histological features include epidermal atrophy, hyperkeratosis, pigment incontinence, and a lymphocytic perivascular infiltrate. Serological testing (VDRL, RPR, TPPA) is positive in virtually all patients with late lesions. Differential diagnosis includes vitiligo, leprosy, onchocerciasis, tinea versicolor, and post-inflammatory hypopigmentation. Treatment with benzathine penicillin G 1.2 million units (adult) or 600,000 units (child) intramuscularly achieves clinical and serological cure.

typesClassification.description: Well-demarcated white or pale patches resembling vitiligo; most common late manifestation; often symmetrically distributed on trunk, limbs, and face; may coalesce into large patches.
Dark, hyperpigmented patches mixed with achromic areas; produces a characteristic mottled or 'leopard skin' appearance; may be localised or widespread.
Combined achromic and hyperchromic patches in the same patient; produces a patchwork or mottled skin appearance over trunk, arms, and legs.
Thickened, hyperkeratotic plaques with scaling and fissuring; may affect palms and soles; less common but more symptomatic; can cause functional limitation.
Thin, atrophic skin patches with loss of appendages (sweat glands, hair follicles); less common; may follow hyperkeratotic lesions.
Late pinta lesions colonised by superficial fungi (dermatophytes, candida) causing secondary infection, scaling, and inflammation.

primarySymptoms: Well-demarcated depigmented (white or pale) patches on the trunk, arms, or face that have been present for months to years.
Mottled skin with both pale and dark patches creating a patchwork or 'pinto' appearance (the name 'pinta' derives from the spotted appearance).
Hyperkeratotic, thickened plaques on the palms, soles, elbows, or knees causing pain, cracking, and difficulty with manual work or walking.
Gradual darkening of skin patches with irregular borders on the face, neck, and trunk.
Itching (pruritus) or burning sensation in affected skin patches, particularly in hyperkeratotic lesions.
Skin sensitivity to minor trauma in affected areas with easy bruising and poor wound healing.
Functional limitation when hyperkeratotic lesions affect the palms or soles, making grip or walking painful.
Psychological distress and social stigma from visible skin disfigurement, particularly in communities where the condition carries cultural beliefs about contagion or witchcraft.
Fine scaling or flaking of hyperpigmented patches.
Absence of systemic symptoms (fever, malaise, lymphadenopathy) — late pinta does not cause constitutional illness.
earlyWarningSigns: New depigmented or hyperpigmented skin patches in a patient from an endemic area with no other explanation
Slowly progressive hypopigmented macules that do not respond to standard skin treatments
Pigmentary change combined with hyperkeratosis of palms or soles in a patient with previous skin lesions
Family history of similar skin lesions in a household or community in an endemic area
Serological evidence of treponemal infection in a patient with chronic skin changes
emergencySigns: Secondary bacterial infection of cracked hyperkeratotic lesions with cellulitis — requires oral or IV antibiotics
Severe fissuring of hyperkeratotic palms or soles causing significant pain and functional impairment
Secondary fungal infection of chronic lesions with extensive tinea corporis-like spread
Psychiatric crisis related to severe cosmetic disfigurement and social stigma — requires psychological support

causes.cause: Chronic Treponema carateum infection (years to decades untreated)
causes.description: Pinta is caused by Treponema carateum, a spirochaete transmitted by direct non-sexual skin-to-skin contact. After primary and secondary lesions (which may be mild or unnoticed), the spirochaete persists in the skin for decades, causing chronic inflammatory changes and dyschromia. The mechanism of pigment disturbance involves destruction of melanocytes by chronic inflammation and melanin incontinence (melanin in the dermis taken up by macrophages).

causes.cause: Non-venereal transmission in conditions of poverty and overcrowding
causes.description: Pinta is transmitted by direct skin-to-skin contact between individuals in the same household or community, facilitated by skin abrasions, sharing of clothing, and poor hygiene. It is not sexually transmitted. Transmission is more common in children and young adults. The disease is limited to remote, underserved communities where healthcare access is limited.

causes.cause: Chronic inflammatory response to persistent treponemal infection
causes.description: The chronic inflammation in late pinta causes epidermal atrophy, loss of adnexal structures (sweat glands, hair follicles), and pigment incontinence. The dyschromia results from both melanocyte destruction (achromic areas) and melanin overproduction and dumping (hyperchromic areas).

causes.cause: Geographical and environmental factors maintaining endemicity
causes.description: Pinta remains endemic in isolated rural communities of Central and South America where treponemal infections are not systematically treated. Limited healthcare access, poverty, and lack of public health infrastructure allow the disease cycle to continue. Active case-finding and mass treatment campaigns have dramatically reduced prevalence in some areas.

The immune response to T. carateum involves both cellular (CD4+ T-cell mediated) and humoral (antibody) mechanisms. Serological positivity (VDRL, RPR, TPPA) persists lifelong after infection. The chronic skin lesions represent the long-term tissue response to persistent antigenic stimulation.

riskFactors.factor: Living in or originating from endemic areas in Central/South America
riskFactors.description: Remote rural communities in Mexico, Colombia, Venezuela, Ecuador, Peru, and Brazil have the highest risk. The disease is geographically limited to areas where YAWS is also endemic.
Endemic communities are characterised by poverty, limited access to healthcare, and lack of systematic treponemal disease treatment programmes.
Conditions facilitating skin-to-skin transmission: limited access to clean water, shared bedding and clothing, skin abrasions from manual labour.
The non-venereal nature of transmission means children and young adults are primarily affected; unlike syphilis, there is no sexual route of transmission.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of pinta or similar treponemal disease
riskFactors.description: Household contacts of an infected person are at elevated risk due to shared living conditions and direct contact transmission.

affectedDemographics: Primarily affects children and young adults in remote rural communities of Central and South America.
Equal sex distribution; no sexual transmission means no gender disparity.
Most cases reported from Mexico, Colombia, Venezuela, Ecuador, Peru, and Brazil.
Active disease is uncommon in adults over 50, suggesting natural resolution of some cases or under-reporting in older populations.
No ethnic predilection beyond the geographic limitation to endemic areas.
diagnosisOverview: The diagnosis of late lesions of pinta should be considered in any patient from an endemic area presenting with chronic dyschromic skin changes. Clinical assessment includes history (origin, residence in endemic area, prior skin lesions, family history), examination (distribution and morphology of skin lesions), and serological testing. Serology for treponemal infection (VDRL or RPR, confirmed with TPPA or FTA-ABS) is positive in virtually 100% of patients with late pinta lesions. Dark-field microscopy of lesion exudate may show treponemes during active early lesions but is less reliable in late-stage disease where organisms are sparse. Skin biopsy shows characteristic histology: epidermal atrophy, hyperkeratosis, perivascular lymphocytic infiltrate, pigment incontinence with dermal macrophages containing melanin (melanophages), and absence of vasculitis or granulomata. Differential diagnosis includes: vitiligo (depigmented without hyperpigmented areas, no treponemal serology), leprosy (anaesthetic patches, nerve involvement, acid-fast bacilli), onchocerciasis (skin nodules, ocular involvement, microfilariae), tinea versicolor (scaly hypopigmented patches, KOH positive), and post-inflammatory hypopigmentation. Treatment is straightforward and highly effective.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screening test for treponemal infection; positive in virtually all patients with late pinta; quantitative titre guides response to treatment
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood test; results within 24-48 hours; quantitative result (e.g., 1:4, 1:8)

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms treponemal infection; remains positive lifelong; distinguishes current from past treated infection
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood test; results within 1-2 weeks; positive confirms treponemal infection

diagnosticTests.purpose: Visualises treponemes in active early lesions; less reliable in late-stage chronic lesions where organisms are sparse
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Scraping from active lesion edge examined under dark-field microscope; immediate result

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms diagnosis and excludes other causes of dyschromia (leprosy, onchocerciasis, vitiligo); shows characteristic pigment incontinence and lymphocytic infiltrate
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Punch biopsy under local anesthesia; results in 5-7 days

diagnosticTests.test: Wood's lamp examination of skin lesions
diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses fluorescence pattern of hypopigmented patches; may help distinguish pinta from tinea versicolor or vitiligo
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: UV lamp examination in dark room; immediate

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes secondary dermatophyte infection of hyperkeratotic lesions
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Skin scraping; immediate result under microscopy

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms absence of CNS involvement in late pinta (unlike syphilis); screening for tabes dorsalis or general paralysis is not required
Late pinta does not cause cardiovascular involvement; cardiac assessment is not routinely indicated unless symptoms suggest unrelated pathology

treatmentOverview: Treatment of late lesions of pinta is highly effective with penicillin, though skin pigmentation changes may be slow to resolve or may be permanent. Benzathine penicillin G 1.2 million units (adults) or 600,000 units (children under 10 years) by intramuscular injection is the WHO-recommended single-dose treatment, providing levels above the minimum treponemicidal concentration for approximately 2 weeks. Alternative regimens for penicillin-allergic patients include: doxycycline 100mg twice daily orally for 14 days, or tetracycline 500mg four times daily for 14 days. Clinical improvement (resolution of hyperkeratosis, reduction in scaling) typically occurs within weeks to months of treatment. Pigmentary changes (achromic and hyperchromic patches) may improve slowly over 6-12 months or may be permanent, particularly in longstanding lesions. Serological response is expected: VDRL/RPR titre should fall 4-fold within 12 months of treatment. A Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction (fever, rigors, hypotension) may occur within 2-4 hours of treatment in some patients; it is usually mild and self-limiting in pinta. Contact tracing and treatment of household contacts and community members is essential to interrupt transmission.

medicalTreatments.name: Benzathine penicillin G 1.2 million units IM (single dose, adult)
medicalTreatments.description: WHO-recommended first-line treatment for pinta; provides treponemicidal levels for approximately 2 weeks; highly effective even in late-stage disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical cure in >95%; serological cure in majority; pigmentary changes may be slow to resolve or permanent

medicalTreatments.name: Benzathine penicillin G 600,000 units IM (single dose, child)
medicalTreatments.description: Paediatric dose; same efficacy as adult dose; well-tolerated; given as a single injection into the buttock.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Equivalent to adult dosing in children; cure rates >95%

medicalTreatments.name: Doxycycline 100mg twice daily orally for 14 days (penicillin-allergic patients)
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative for penicillin-allergic patients; effective against treponemes; doxycycline also covers likely co-endemic conditions (rickettsial infections, Lyme disease co-endemic areas).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical cure rates similar to penicillin; ensure compliance for full 14-day course

medicalTreatments.name: Tetracycline 500mg four times daily orally for 14 days (penicillin-allergic patients)
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative for patients unable to take penicillin or doxycycline; similar efficacy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective but higher pill burden; less commonly used than doxycycline

medicalTreatments.name: Topical corticosteroid (clobetasol 0.05% ointment twice daily) for hyperkeratotic lesions
medicalTreatments.description: Reduces inflammation, scaling, and fissuring in hyperkeratotic late lesions; adjunctive to penicillin.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improves symptoms in 60-70%; does not replace systemic antibiotic treatment

medicalTreatments.name: Emollient moisturisers for dry, scaling skin
medicalTreatments.description: Petroleum jelly or urea-based emollients applied 2-3 times daily to affected skin; reduces scaling and improves skin barrier.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improves skin comfort and barrier function; non-specific benefit

medicalTreatments.name: Oral antihistamines (cetirizine 10mg daily) for pruritus
medicalTreatments.description: Symptomatic relief of itching in hyperkeratotic and dyschromic lesions.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces pruritus in 50-60% of patients; non-sedating antihistamines preferred

medicalTreatments.name: Oral antifungal therapy (terbinafine or itraconazole) for secondary dermatophyte infection
medicalTreatments.description: For secondary tinea infection of hyperkeratotic lesions; oral therapy preferred over topical due to extent of involvement.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cures secondary fungal infection in 80-90% with 2-4 week courses

surgicalOptions.name: Skin biopsy for diagnostic purposes
surgicalOptions.description: Punch biopsy of affected skin confirms diagnosis and excludes other causes of dyschromia.

surgicalOptions.name: Cosmetic camouflage for disfiguring skin changes (not surgical)
surgicalOptions.description: Dermatological camouflage using concealing cosmetics for achromic and hyperchromic patches; improves cosmetic appearance and reduces stigma.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Effective for cosmetic improvement; does not treat underlying disease; requires specialist camouflage training

alternativeTreatments.name: Community mass treatment campaigns (WHO strategy)
alternativeTreatments.description: Single-dose benzathine penicillin G for all members of endemic communities regardless of clinical status; interrupts transmission and reduces prevalence dramatically.

alternativeTreatments.name: Health education and hygiene improvement in endemic communities
alternativeTreatments.description: Community education about pinta transmission, promotion of personal hygiene, and improvement of living conditions reduces transmission.

alternativeTreatments.name: Psychological support for patients with cosmetic disfigurement
alternativeTreatments.description: Counselling and community support to address the social stigma and psychological distress associated with visible skin changes.

whySeeSpecialist: Late pinta lesions require specialist assessment by an infectious disease or tropical medicine physician to confirm the diagnosis with serology, exclude other causes of dyschromia (leprosy, onchocerciasis), initiate appropriate treatment, and coordinate community public health measures. Dermatology input is helpful for management of hyperkeratotic lesions and cosmetic concerns.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek an infectious disease or tropical medicine centre with experience in treponemal diseases and access to serological testing and treatment. In endemic countries, regional health posts with access to WHO treatment protocols are the first point of contact. Ask about experience with pinta specifically and contact-tracing protocols.
hospitalCriteria: Infectious disease or tropical medicine service with treponemal disease experience
Serological laboratory for VDRL, RPR, and treponemal confirmatory testing
Dermatology service for management of hyperkeratotic and dyschromic lesions
Public health and community medicine for contact tracing and mass treatment campaigns
Access to WHO essential medicines including benzathine penicillin
keyFacilities: Dermatology outpatient clinic with hyperkeratosis management
Community health programme for endemic area case-finding
preventionStrategies: Mass treatment campaigns in endemic communities following WHO strategy: single-dose benzathine penicillin G for all community members regardless of clinical status.
Health education in endemic communities about pinta transmission and importance of treatment.
Contact tracing and treatment of household members and close contacts of identified patients.
Improved living conditions and hygiene in endemic communities to reduce transmission.
No effective pre-exposure prophylaxis exists; prevention relies on community treatment and education.
lifestyleModifications: Maintain good skin hygiene with regular washing and emollient use to reduce scaling and secondary infection.
Avoid skin-to-skin contact with affected individuals until they have completed treatment.
Protect affected skin from trauma, which may worsen hyperkeratotic lesions.
Wear protective gloves and footwear if hyperkeratotic lesions affect hands or feet to prevent fissuring.
Seek psychological support if skin changes cause significant distress or social stigma.

recommended: Balanced diet with adequate protein to support skin health and wound healing
Vitamins A and E for skin repair (carotenoid-rich foods, nuts, seeds)
Adequate hydration to maintain skin moisture
Iron-rich diet if anaemia from chronic infection
avoid: No specific dietary restrictions for pinta itself
Alcohol excess during doxycycline treatment (hepatotoxicity risk)
Excessive sun exposure on depigmented patches (increased sunburn risk in achromic areas)

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise is unrestricted; hyperkeratotic lesions on palms and soles may limit some activities (grip, prolonged walking) until treated. Normal activity resumes after antibiotic treatment and lesion improvement.
dailyManagement: Take penicillin injection as prescribed; the single dose is curative.
Apply topical corticosteroid and emollient to affected skin twice daily after bathing.
Keep affected skin clean and dry; change clothing daily.
Report signs of secondary bacterial infection (increased redness, warmth, pus, fever) to health worker.
Attend follow-up at 3, 6, and 12 months for clinical and serological assessment.
prognosis: With appropriate penicillin treatment, late lesions of pinta have an excellent prognosis for clinical cure. Hyperkeratotic lesions improve within weeks to months of treatment, and secondary infections resolve. Pigmentary changes may improve slowly over 6-12 months or may be permanent, particularly in longstanding lesions where melanocytes have been irreversibly damaged. Late pinta does not cause cardiovascular or nervous system disease, distinguishing it favourably from late syphilis. Serological titres fall progressively after treatment, and most patients achieve a 4-fold or greater reduction in VDRL/RPR within 12 months. Relapse after adequate treatment is extremely rare. The main burden of late pinta is cosmetic disfigurement and social stigma in affected communities, which requires community education and psychological support alongside medical treatment.
recoveryTimeline: Clinical improvement (reduction in hyperkeratosis, scaling, and fissuring) typically occurs within 2-4 weeks of penicillin treatment. Skin inflammation resolves over 1-3 months. Pigmentary changes (achromic and hyperchromic patches) may begin to improve after 3-6 months but may take 12-24 months to stabilise, and some changes may be permanent. Serological response lags behind clinical response; VDRL/RPR titres fall gradually over 12 months. Complete resolution of dyschromia is variable and depends on lesion duration.
complications: Secondary bacterial infection of cracked hyperkeratotic lesions — managed with antibiotics and wound care; prevented with emollient use and skin protection.
Secondary fungal infection of chronic lesions — managed with oral antifungal therapy; common in tropical climates.
Persistent dyschromia causing cosmetic disfigurement and social stigma — managed with cosmetic camouflage, psychological support, and community education.
Functional limitation from hyperkeratotic lesions of palms and soles — improved with penicillin treatment and topical steroids; severe cases may require job modification.
Psychological distress from visible skin disease — requires community support, education, and psychological counselling.
Misdiagnosis as leprosy leading to patient stigmatisation — prevented with accurate serological diagnosis.
Re-infection in endemic community without mass treatment coverage — prevented with community-wide treatment campaigns.

supportResources.name: WHO — Yaws and other endemic treponematoses
supportResources.description: WHO programme for yaws and pinta eradication; provides treatment guidelines, surveillance protocols, and technical support for endemic countries.

supportResources.name: PAHO — Pinta and other treponematoses in the Americas
supportResources.description: Pan American Health Organization providing regional guidance on treponemal disease control in Central and South America.

supportResources.name: DermNet NZ — Pinta information
supportResources.description: Dermatology educational resource with clinical images and information on pinta and other treponemal skin diseases.

supportResources.name: MSF / Doctors Without Borders — tropical dermatology resources
supportResources.description: Provides dermatology care in endemic regions and resources for managing neglected tropical skin diseases including pinta.

confusedWithConditions.name: Vitiligo
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Vitiligo causes depigmented patches without hyperpigmented areas and has no treponemal association. Serological testing (VDRL, TPPA) is negative in vitiligo and positive in pinta. The distribution pattern differs: vitiligo often affects face, hands, and acral areas symmetrically.

confusedWithConditions.name: Leprosy (Hansen's Disease)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Leprosy causes hypopigmented patches with anaesthesia (loss of sensation) and nerve involvement (thickened peripheral nerves). Skin biopsy shows acid-fast bacilli (Mycobacterium leprae), not treponemes. Serology does not distinguish pinta from leprosy but clinical features do.

confusedWithConditions.name: Onchocerciasis (River Blindness)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Onchocerciasis causes skin nodules (onchocercomata), lichenification, and depigmentation ('leopard skin') and is associated with ocular involvement and microfilariae on skin snip. Geographic distribution overlaps with pinta in some areas; skin snip examination distinguishes the two.

confusedWithConditions.name: Tinea Versicolor (Pityriasis Versicolor)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Tinea versicolor causes hypopigmented or hyperpigmented scaly patches; KOH preparation shows 'spaghetti and meatballs' fungal elements; VDRL/TPPA negative. Distribution on trunk and upper arms with fine scaling distinguishes it from pinta.

relatedConditions.name: Yaws
relatedConditions.relevance: Yaws (T. pallidum subspecies pertenue) is co-endemic with pinta in the same rural communities and has overlapping clinical features. Both respond to benzathine penicillin. Distinction between the two is less important for treatment than identification of treponemal disease for community treatment campaigns.

relatedConditions.name: Syphilis
relatedConditions.relevance: Syphilis (T. pallidum subspecies pallidum) shares the same serological profile as pinta but has very different clinical manifestations and transmission routes. Syphilis involves cardiovascular and CNS systems in late stage; pinta does not.

relatedConditions.name: Endemic Non-Venereal Treponematosis (General)
relatedConditions.relevance: Pinta, yaws, and bejel are the three non-venereal treponematoses grouped together for WHO eradication campaigns; they share treatment and public health strategies.

faqs.question: What is pinta?
faqs.answer: Pinta is a chronic non-venereal treponemal skin disease caused by Treponema carateum, limited to remote rural communities of Central and South America. It causes skin depigmentation and hyperpigmentation (dyschromia) and is transmitted by direct skin-to-skin contact.

faqs.question: Is pinta contagious?
faqs.answer: Yes, pinta is contagious via direct skin-to-skin contact in non-sexual settings, particularly among children and household contacts. However, it is not sexually transmitted. Treatment of the affected person stops transmission.

faqs.question: What causes late lesions of pinta?
faqs.answer: Late lesions of pinta develop years to decades after untreated initial infection. They are characterised by chronic inflammatory changes in the skin causing pigmentary disturbance (achromic and hyperchromic patches), hyperkeratosis, and atrophy.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of late pinta?
faqs.answer: Late lesions of pinta include well-demarcated depigmented (white) or hyperpigmented (dark) patches, mottled skin, hyperkeratotic plaques on palms and soles, scaling, and sometimes itching. Systemic symptoms are absent.

faqs.question: How is pinta diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis of late pinta involves clinical assessment (skin lesions in a patient from an endemic area), serological testing (VDRL/RPR positive, confirmed with TPPA), and skin biopsy showing characteristic pigment incontinence and lymphocytic infiltrate.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for late lesions of pinta?
faqs.answer: A single intramuscular injection of benzathine penicillin G (1.2 million units for adults, 600,000 for children) is the WHO-recommended treatment and cures the infection. Hyperkeratotic lesions improve within weeks; pigmentary changes may take months to resolve or may be permanent.

faqs.question: Can late lesions of pinta be reversed?
faqs.answer: The treponemal infection is cured with penicillin, and hyperkeratotic lesions resolve. However, pigmentary changes (achromic and hyperchromic patches) may be permanent, particularly if longstanding. New lesions do not develop after treatment.

faqs.question: Is pinta related to syphilis?
faqs.answer: Pinta is caused by a different treponeme (T. carateum) than syphilis (T. pallidum subspecies pallidum), but all treponemal infections produce the same serological profile. Unlike syphilis, pinta does not involve the cardiovascular or nervous systems and is not sexually transmitted.

faqs.question: Where is pinta found?
faqs.answer: Pinta is limited to remote rural communities in Central and South America, particularly Mexico, Colombia, Venezuela, Ecuador, Peru, and Brazil. It is not found in other parts of the world.

faqs.question: What does 'pinto' mean in the context of this disease?
faqs.answer: The word 'pinto' means spotted or mottled in Spanish, describing the characteristic patchwork appearance of dyschromic skin patches in late pinta — pale and dark areas mixed together, like a spotted animal.

faqs.question: How is pinta different from yaws?
faqs.answer: Both pinta and yaws are non-venereal treponematoses endemic in the same rural communities and share treatment (benzathine penicillin). Yaws causes papillomatous lesions and periostitis; pinta causes dyschromic skin changes. Both can coexist in the same community.

faqs.question: Does pinta cause nerve damage?
faqs.answer: No. Unlike syphilis and leprosy, late pinta does not involve the peripheral nerves or central nervous system. The disease is confined to the skin.

faqs.question: Can you get pinta from swimming or shared water?
faqs.answer: No. Pinta is transmitted by direct skin-to-skin contact, not through water. Swimming pools, rivers, and shared bathing facilities are not sources of transmission.

faqs.question: Is pinta the same as vitiligo?
faqs.answer: No. Vitiligo causes only depigmented patches without hyperpigmented areas, has no treponemal cause, and does not produce positive VDRL/TPPA. Pinta causes both depigmented and hyperpigmented patches and is confirmed by positive treponemal serology.

faqs.question: How long after treatment do symptoms improve?
faqs.answer: Hyperkeratotic lesions and scaling improve within 2-4 weeks of penicillin treatment. Pigmentary changes may begin to improve after 3-6 months but may take 12-24 months to stabilise; some changes may be permanent.

faqs.question: Is pinta life-threatening?
faqs.answer: No. Pinta is not life-threatening. The disease is limited to the skin and does not involve internal organs, cardiovascular system, or nervous system. The main impact is cosmetic disfigurement and social stigma.

faqs.question: Can household contacts be treated to prevent pinta?
faqs.answer: Yes. All household contacts and close contacts of a patient with pinta should be examined and treated with a single dose of benzathine penicillin G to prevent transmission and disease.

faqs.question: Does pinta affect children differently than adults?
faqs.answer: Primary lesions (pintides) typically occur in childhood. Late lesions manifest in adolescence or adulthood. The disease course and treatment response are similar across age groups.

faqs.question: Is there a vaccine for pinta?
faqs.answer: No vaccine exists for pinta or other treponemal diseases. Prevention relies on mass treatment campaigns, health education, and contact tracing.

faqs.question: What happens if pinta is not treated?
faqs.answer: Untreated pinta follows a chronic course with progressive dyschromic skin changes over years. While not life-threatening, the cosmetic disfigurement worsens, and hyperkeratotic lesions may cause functional impairment. Treatment at any stage is effective.

metaTitle: Late Lesions of Pinta: Causes, Symptoms & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Late lesions of pinta: chronic treponemal skin disease, dyschromia, benzathine penicillin treatment, endemic Central America. Medically reviewed guide.
